posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Just read Neal Martin’s recent article on mature Burgundy. Great contribution. He needs to write more of this. I now realise why it is useful to remain a subscriber to VINOUS. In the past I regularly complained about the lack of covering mature Burgundy. Such articles really help us in finding out when to consume such great wiines. I trust Neal’s palate. He enjoys the wines younger than say Alan Meadows who considers his wines to be ready when everybody is already in the grave…Sorry Alan. Neal : keep up the good work. One remark : BONNES ARES 2015 by G ROUMIER already ready??..I’ll find out soon.
SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
I am glad that you like to read Neal Martin’s TNs regarding matured Burgundy.

Here are my comments :

(1) maturity of any burgundy wines is hard to define - as each burgundy lover has his own standard of maturity. Your maturity may not be mine;

(2) after reading the TNs on the maturity of a certain wine, for example, Bonnes Mares 2015 by G. Roumier. written by Neal Martin, your mind will register his descriptions and will remember them; and

(3) here may lie a problem : the precept of Bossuet : ** The greatest disorder of a human mind is to believe things are so because you wish them to be so **

Johan …you are always my wine friend and please do not interpret as an offence*.

. .

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
No offence taken dear Peter. So glad to hear from you again.

Greetings JOHAN

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Friends,
I just wanted to make clear I appreciate articles on mature Burgundy like the late great CLIVE COATES did with his 1O year on tastings. Of course I realise that one person’s palate does not equal another one’s palate. But never mind. It is in my opinion important for a wine magazine to not report on recent vintages but also to do somefollow-up. Especially for famous regions such as BURGUNDY. I missed that in Wine Advocate and stoppend subscribing. Like VINOUS much better…
SINCERELY JOHAN

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Drink them whenever you think you will enjoy them and absolutely treat anyone else’s ‘drinking window’ as rough guidance at best (and pure nonsense at worst). FWIW I put my own drinking windows into CT for wines I have, but am never afraid to ignore my own advice

I always liked the way Aussie wine critic Jeremy Oliver handled drinking windows. What looked like the usual overly precise dates, when you looked closed showed there were only about 8 brackets, which went something like (from vintage):1-2 years, 3-5 years, 6-10 years, 11-16 years etc. So all he was effectively saying was: very short, short, medium, moderately long, long, very long. That coupled with a very focused area of tasting, meant he was better able than the generalists to use past performance to inform future potential. Not that I agreed with his dates (a bit on the short side for my preferences), nor his tastes (as with other Aussie critics, too warm South Australian focused).

One swallow doesn’t make a summer (nor a cellaring strategy), but I loved the evening when I brought a 1969 Red Burgundy round to a Burg-loving friend’s house. It was stunningly complex, and even more impressive in that it wasn’t a highly sought after producer, nor a highly prestigious appellation. It evoked a genuine worry that he’d been drinking his red burgundy way too young. That’s the luck of older wines though, as the highs can be incredibly high, but there can also be plenty of times where we think it would have been better a decade earlier.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Johan–thanks for your comment. My friends an I have been hammering on Steve Tanzer, then Antonio Galloni and Neal Martin, to report on burgundies with age for years. For quite a while, John Gilman was really to only one doing it, and this has tapered off as well, as I understand it. Every once in a while we’d get a trickle of reports. Neal’s report is by far the most extensive, and very welcome. It’s not as if they don’t drink the wines–we’ve just been hoping that now and then they would drop a report of what they’ve experienced.

My other point is that it’s not just about when the wines are “ready”. It’s also about whether initial opinions about the vintage have been borne out with time, and whether exalted producers who received high scores, in fact, have had their reputations upheld. And borderline producers–did they surprise with time or disappoint? Oh, and of course, are the reviewers opinions, based upon tasting large numbers of samples, still accurate and predictive when re-assessed 12-15 years later. Etc.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: Not sure about these days, but one way to see this was to subscribe to Allen Meadows and look at old stuff in his database. All these guys get to drink a lot of older wines, and Meadows would create database entries for lots of things that didn’t show up in the regular reports.

posts.name: Gaudissabois Johan
posts.cooked:
Dear William,

Thank you for answering. I must, however, add that I am not looking for one-offs (one tasting note on a certain wine…) but for entire articles as Clive published and Neal recently did. Do you contribute such articles???
Greetings JOHAN

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Interesting comments for chewing and also cutting it with an industrial strength drill.

One of the my enjoyment of drinking red burgundy is to open a bottle ( of the same wine ) once every few months or so. For example - for older vintages (1) Audoin-Marsannay-La Charme aux Prêtres 05 (1119-8745 $37.50); and Vougeraie – Bel Air 08 (1228-2068 $55.25 ). For newer vintages : Dom Chevalier - Corton - Rognet 13(1473-1023 $131.) ; Gabin et Félix Richoux - Irancy Les Cailles 15 ( 1494-6526 $49.75) and M. Mallard -CdNuits Village 16 (1475-2043 $49).

It is a fascinating experience to check how each of them …aging and changing.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Of course, generally twice a year for Burgundy, twice a year for Bordeaux.

Last year we had two installments on old Burgundy, Robert Parker Wine Advocate and Robert Parker Wine Advocate in March and November, with wines from the 2000s all the way back to the 1930s.

I’m working on a new installment now, as it happens.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: William I’m going to make a suggestion. Vinous sends a weekly email announcing what articles are coming out the next week. This is very helpful in flagging something that might be of interest to me. I never know when anything is being published in the WA that I might want to read.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: WA sends an email whenever new content is published.

posts.name: Nick Gangas

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
At this point in my wine buying career I am pretty much no longer buying recent vintages of Burgundy, seeing as almost all of my cellar is Burgundy. As a result, I subscribe to various online journals (Vinous, WA, Gilman, Burghound) pretty much only for tasting notes on older vintages. Unfortunately, given my focus, it is frustrating waiting for such notes to drop 1-2 times a year. John Gilman, whose View from the Cellar was initially almost entirely devoted to a…wait for it…a view from the cellar… has admitted that he doesn’t get the opportunity to drink as much older Burgundy these days, and like most journals the marketplace of potential subscribers probably demands an emphasis on recent releases. I only subscribe to Burghound for his very useful Database section on recently tasted older Burgs, but rather than releasing fewer notes more frequently, they at least in recent years seem to drop those notes a couple of times per year. I have been part of the chorus on the Vinous boards pushing for years for more tasting notes on older
Burgs, and so the latest NM article was great to see. And I only resubscribed to the WA after many years solely to read William’s notes on Burgundy, and particularly the Up From the Cellar articles…but I wish they dropped more than twice a year…yes, I know, never satisfied. And I do find the WA site harder to navigate, and had no idea you could get email notifications of new articles, 90% of which I would probably skip anyway.
I just wish these notes were published by all of these sources, in a more casual manner (ie: not saved for thematic articles) on a monthly or bimonthly schedule…”here are the Burgs I tasted in the past two months”.
